data.title: SL Green Realty: The Market Is Still Too Pessimistic While Fundamentals Improve
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

SL Green Realty (SLG), Manhattan’s largest office landlord, currently trades at some of the lowest levels since the Financial Crisis, and despite doubling since 2023, the stock still seems to offer a solid opportunity alongside the company’s preferred.

Especially now, after a solid earnings report released recently and expecting the momentum of their recovery to continue, I believe the risk-reward is overall worth a Buy rating, with a very solid dividend and potential to improve together with the environment, despite near-term macro uncertainty.

SLG’s latest report showed some significant improvements compared to the previous quarter and Q3’24 in terms of FAD (~$75.5 million in Q3), although the 9M level is lower compared to a year ago since last year was boosted by significant non-cash reserve add-backs (didn’t recur), while 2025 came with higher cash deductions for tenant improvements and joint venture leasing costs, in part due to significant improvements such as the “3 huge leases” that made One Madison’s occupancy jump to 91.2%.

Recently, SLG announced that its office leasing volume reached 2.3 million square feet YTD (as of early December 2025), placing them on track to meet their target of 93.2% (vs. a solid 92.4% as of Q3) in same-store office occupancy by the end of the year while also maintaining their pipeline of ~1.2 million square feet.

Not accounting for this improvement, we’re looking at an annualized FAD of ~$283.4 million when annualizing the 9M results, which is in line with the levels from a few years ago, which would place them at a P/FAD ratio of ~11.85.

The market didn’t react that well to the company’s 2026 guidance despite the improvements seen in the 2025 one, as this was below expectations. Still, we’re looking at a decent 3.5% to 4.5% cash NOI growth, while their 2027 guidance assumes over 10% same-store cash NOI growth, which would be exceptional if true, but it will depend on many other factors, as I’ll mention below.

Financially, based on SLG’s latest report, we see a solid position for a REIT, with about $187 million in cash and cash equivalents, while the assets are well above their liabilities. The company is actively aiming to reduce their leverage, projecting a further $33 million reduction in interest in 2027, while the debt remains spread relatively well over the next >5 years, with a ~4.79% weighted average coupon.

As for the equity, we see about $3.08 billion when taking out the total non-controlling and preferred equity, which is not far from the company’s market cap of $3.42 billion, and that’s despite the assets being significantly depreciated and not reflecting their real value since they’re recorded at the price of acquisition, not at current market values—which could improve in the future—so we’re likely looking at a solid discount.

On December 5, 2025, SLG also announced the closing of its SLG Opportunistic Debt Fund with over $1.3 billion in commitments, well above the company’s initial $1 billion target. They did this as a way to capitalize on what they call the “dislocation” in the market, with many NYC building owners struggling to refinance loans and the company stepping in to fund them for very attractive rates, getting asset management fees instead of the traditional rent. As the company says in the press release:

We believe this milestone underscores the strong and sustained demand from a global investor base seeking access to SL Green’s differentiated real estate credit platform and its proven ability to generate attractive risk-adjusted returns. (...) Our focus now shifts to deployment into a robust pipeline of debt investment opportunities in a rapidly evolving market environment.

Of course, distressed real estate is far from the safest thing out there, but this could be a solid time for the company to accelerate their actions meant to take advantage of the environment, as the company is now in a stage of investing ahead of the curve, with significant asset reshuffling since the pandemic, including the acquisition of the remaining interest at 800 Third Avenue for $5.1 million recently. As a note, this fund is expected to add about $13.7 million to the company’s 2026 FFO, which seems like a very reasonable assumption given its size.

Dividends

After cutting it in recent years to adapt to the environment, SLG currently pays a ~6.86% dividend yield ($3.09 per share), which was recently changed from monthly to quarterly. This costs about $236.17 million per year (assuming no increase), meaning a payout of about 83.33%, which is not bad and should leave some room for hikes, especially if the environment improves in the future.

There’s also the company’s cumulative preferred stock (SLG.PR.I) with a 6.5% initial dividend rate that’s currently paying a 7.55% yield, with a par value of $25 per share compared to the current $21.62. This has been callable since 2017, but the company didn’t really find it necessary to settle it so far, and right now, with the stock “costing” the equivalent of a ~7% yield, it wouldn’t make sense to redeem the preferred over the common. And even if they do that for some reason, we’d still get a ~16% profit plus any dividends, so this looks like a solid one to consider as well for a safer alternative.

Risks & Opportunities

Currently, SLG is facing several major issues that stem from their asset class and business model. First of all, we have the classic issues caused by high interest rates, forcing the company and its tenants into weaker financial positions. This is normal for the asset class, as REITs depend on their ability to issue external capital (be it actual debt issuance or equity) to fund their acquisitions unless they want to sell assets, which isn’t a smart idea either in a weak market.

This is why rate cuts could potentially be great for the entire asset class, as they could lead to lower debt costs and a decrease in the risk-free rate, resulting in the market having to “risk up” if it wants better yields, which is similar to the environment we saw a decade ago when SLG was trading even above $120 per share.

However, keep in mind that rate cuts aren’t enough to push debt costs lower, as the market is still showing significant volatility. Recently, we’ve seen the market pricing some uncertainty around the new Fed Chair (Powell’s term ends in May 2026), and even though this role only has 1 vote out of 12 in FOMC rate decisions, keep in mind that this person’s words and stances have a significant impact on the market’s expectations.

If the new Chair just obediently pushes for what Trump wants during such an environment, we could see the market’s opinion get affected even more during already tense times.

Then, we have the specific issues related to the company’s industry, as the pandemic caused some (at least temporary) disruptions due to the stay-at-home trends that made companies realize it could be a good way to avoid paying significant rent in some cases. As I said in the Highwoods Properties (HIW) analysis posted a couple of months ago, this entire industry is facing this kind of headwind, so it’s not something affecting only SLG, which still shows surprisingly good occupancy rates compared to the rest of the industry.

Again, on top of the work-from-home or hybrid trends we’ve seen since the pandemic (unless it’s overblown), the reality is that the market is adapting to the new conditions, with weak occupancy rates being met with new construction in Manhattan (and across the country) effectively freezing due to high interest rates and rising construction costs. This is one of the major catalysts in the industry, especially since high-quality Manhattan space is very limited, and SLG is a key player in the market that’s even “overspecialized” in it.

Then, we have the perceived uncertainty around New York’s new mayor, with the market likely pricing in new taxes, residential rent freezes (SLG has some mixed-use properties), potentially affecting SLG directly together with its tenants that may be inclined to leave if things get too expensive.

However, the market should also remember the power that the Mayor of New York actually has, being significantly constrained by Albany's Governor and the State Legislature, which hold the “last word” (alongside the Financial Control Board in case his policies unbalance the NYC budget) that could act as a significant moderation to Mamdani's proposals—and it wouldn’t be the first time a politician isn’t able to meet their promises.

And, even without these extra layers of protection, keep in mind that the city relies heavily on office property taxes (about 20% of its tax revenues), so they’re technically one of his biggest and most important allies, and hurting this industry would only make it harder for him to meet his targets. If anything, the city should consider developing alternative sources of income alongside it instead of hurting one of its pillars at a time when the industry is already weakened.

Regardless of what the mayor will do, the company’s significant concentration is ultimately always a risk to keep in mind, and it’s something that I’d always want to get at a solid premium just to account for it—which wasn’t the case a decade ago, for example.

There’s also SLG’s bid for the Times Square Casino, although that project was almost dead even before Mamdani's arrival, as it didn’t receive the Community Advisory Committee’s approval back in September 2025. Although the company’s CEO initially said it’s "not completely dead" and hopes that it could get a re-vote, the licences were won already by the other bidders in December, leaving no license for SL Green in Manhattan.

Risks Reiterated

Even though we’re potentially looking at what the company calls a bottom a couple of years ago and now a potential rebound in the industry thanks to fundamental shifts, we’re still looking at plenty of uncertainty. Even if that may appear to be priced in, there’s nothing that guarantees that things will improve, as the company now faces renewed political uncertainty, while rate cuts won’t be enough to solve these issues.

Meanwhile, their debt will get refinanced at high rates, while the stock is also down to levels that still imply very high issuing costs, forcing the company to go for tools like the distressed debt fund they announced, as getting cap rates that would beat their debt costs is very hard in this market—and things can definitely get worse before they improve.

Valuation

For a FAD-based model, starting with the annualized FAD of $283.4 million, let’s assume a 5% CAGR over the first 5 years to take into account a potential rebound that’s likely below the company’s expectations, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and we'll go for a 12% cost of equity since I'm starting with the FAD on 76.43 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report. This gets us to an intrinsic value of $45.53 per share, which is almost identical to the current ~$45.07 at the moment of writing this.

However, although this model basically estimates that the cash flow should be worth a value around the current level, the company’s assets and interest rate sensitivity can lead to much better prices if they sell them or rate cuts lower the interest, so getting this at a conservative fair value adjustment should leave plenty of room for upside.

Conclusions

I rate SLG and its preferred stock a Buy, as the overall risk-reward looks solid and currently prices in a lot of negatives, while there is plenty of room for improvements given the market they operate in. There are several risks to keep in mind, of course, but the current price seems like an overall good opportunity to get a piece of Manhattan’s largest office landlord and its legacy assets.

data.title: Marqeta: GAAP Profitability Is Priced In
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Marqeta (MQ) is a fintech company that issues virtual and physical cards for consumer-facing platforms like Block (XYZ), DoorDash (DASH), and Affirm (AFRM). These payment cards come agnostic to any bank or merchant a consumer would prefer to facilitate a transaction from. One of the company's goals for 2026 is to reach GAAP profitability. Judging by their earnings outperformance on a quarterly basis, the goal is certainly achievable, and this is one metric most investors and analysts that are following this name have their eyes on. When we look at the 3-year total return chart on the right, we can see that MQ lost 22% of its value in this 3-year period, massively underperforming the (FINX) ETF and the (SPY), which both returned 61.11% and 84.73%, respectively. But year to date (the chart on the left), MQ returned more than 33%, outperforming both the indices and its peers. The outperformance was massive during the second quarter of 2025 but has since slowed down as the stock declined by 25% from the peak of August. On a comparative basis, MQ trades at fair value relative to its peers and sector. Even if management could hit the GAAP profitability target early next year, from a sales multiple basis, I am still not confident enough to place a bid right now, as I don't see any upside potential. Given how volatile the stock has been recently, I suggest if we remain neutral and patient, the market could present a good entry point.

Financial Analysis

Q3 2025 Income Statement

Looking at MQ's latest earnings results, revenue increased 28% YoY to $163.31 million. This growth was bolstered by $155.77 million from the platform's service revenue, $7.53 million from other services, and a total processing volume (TPV) of $98 billion, a 33% YoY increase. Resulting in a revenue beat by $14.90 million. Cost of revenue increased 29% to $48.75 million, resulting in gross profits growing 27% to $114.56 million and landing gross margins at 70%. Adjusted EBITDA grew to $30 million and returned 19% in margins. Operating expenses decreased by 6% to $124.93 million; the decline was mainly driven by compensation and benefits decreasing 16% to $84.87 million and was offset by growth in other items of operational expenses. Comps and benefits account for 68% of overall operating expenses and eat up roughly 52% of total revenue. So this is the main lever management needs to pull for MQ to reach GAAP profitability. Operating losses decreased 75% YoY from $42.2 million to $10.37 million.

On an adjusted basis (when you remove the impact of comps and benefits), MQ is profitable, hence the realization of $30 million of EBITDA in Q3. But GAAP standards require us to report compensation as a real expense. So when management says they expect to realize GAAP profitability next year, they mean that they'll be putting effort into reducing the SBC impact on GAAP profitability. Thus far, they've done an impressive job in that. Net loss has decreased 87% from $28.6 million to $3.62 million, and EPS increased from -$0.06 to -$0.01, beating consensus estimates by $0.01.

Given this growth on the top line and the bottom line, consensus is now estimating that Marqeta could reach GAAP profitability in the second quarter of 2026. This growth was mainly driven by lending, BNPL, and on-demand services. BNPL total processing volume increased by 66% YoY (double the growth of TPV) and by 10 points on a sequential basis, driven by more adoption of pay-anywhere cards.

One fundamental metric I like to pay attention to when trying to evaluate payment processing companies is the take rate. Looking at the net revenue take rate (net revenue / TPV), we can see that prior to the Block's contract renewal, the take was declining modestly from 0.46% to 0.44%. After the renewal, the take rate slightly declined from 0.18% to 0.17%, and it remained flat from there. When we look at the gross profit take rate post the renewal, it has been flat at 0.12%. This flat take rate tells me the company isn't gaining any pricing power as TPV continues to increase. Technically this makes sense, as MQ's clients, like DoorDash, process more transaction volumes through MQ, they transition to a lower pricing tier.

MQ's strategy to improve the bottom line and gain pricing power is by introducing additional services like compliance, funding, fraud, and credit services on top of card issuance and payment processing services. With this effort, the aim is to migrate enough clients to this full-service bundle called "managed by," which has higher gross margins than "powered by."

9 Months 2025 Cash Flow Statement

Looking at the 9-month cash flow statement, we can see that SBC cash add-back massively contributes to the operating cash flow, accounting for roughly 72% of OCF. Even though SBC add-back decreased by 24% YoY, operating cash flow increased 227% to $109.30 million, mainly driven by $17.74 million of cash that was realized from net working capital. This resulted in a positive free cash flow of $7.15 million when we include the impact of SBC. The growth of free cash flow was slightly offset by CAPEX increasing 38% to $23.46 million.

To offset the impact of stock dilution, management has been repurchasing stock from the public market, and as the chart below illustrates, the amount spent on share repurchases has recently been higher than SBC spending. In Q3 the company repurchased 3.2 million shares at the average price of $6.13, and year-to-date purchased 64.6 million shares at the average price of $4.53, reducing shares outstanding by 13%.

Q3 2025 Balance Sheet

Despite a lack of GAAP profitability, Marqeta manages a healthy balance sheet, with cash at $747.2 million (a 16% decline from Q3 of 2024), accounting for more than 30% of MQ's market cap while having zero debt on the liabilities side. The acquisition of TransactPay resulted in restricted cash increasing massively to $234.5 million, offsetting the decrease of current assets to $1.2 billion. Correspondingly, the acquisition added $233.9 million in funds payable, resulting in current liabilities and total liabilities increasing 91% to $636.8 million and $649.2 million, respectively. Total liabilities are still $98 million less than cash reserves; this massive cash balance leaves the firm with enough liquidity to repurchase more shares, which would set a good margin of safety for investors. Only $88 million remains from the buyback program, but I expect the firm will increase the balance if the stock experiences massive drawdowns, as that would present a good entry point for management.

Quantitative Analysis

Looking at the quantitative metrics, MQ was upgraded from a sell to a hold YTD; this upgrade was first due to growth rerating from a B to an A, driven by topline growth performance of 20.19% YoY vs. the sector median growth of 8.36%. Momentum also rerated from an F to a C-, EPS revisions rerated from a C to a C+, and profitability rerated from a C to a B-. Fortunately, the upgrade in other factors didn't result in the stock trading at a massive premium; instead, the valuation moderated from a D- to a D.

Valuation

MQ is among 8 companies in this industry with a D rating for valuation, and in fact, about 56% of stocks in this industry still trade at a premium to the sector median, with only 4 trading at fair value and 12 trading at a discount. On an EV/Sales (TTM) basis, MQ scored a B- as it trades at 2.39 vs. the sector median of 3.07; however, when we look at the price/sales (TTM) multiple, it scores a D+ with 4.12 vs. 3.26. These are not significant disparities from median multiples and where its peers are trading; hence, I think MQ is fairly valued.

Risk

Management stated 2 renewal contracts with clients; one would take effect in Q4 of 2025 and the other in Q1 of 2026. Each with an estimated gross profit drag of 2 points. Furthermore, the partnership of Bancorp with Cash App is estimated to cause another 2-point drag on gross profit growth. All in all, totaling a potential 6-point drag in gross profit next year. The impact here could be a gross margin contraction, which could result in the company missing GAAP profitability in Q2 of 2026, imposing a major risk on the stock price. Another risk that needs to not be understated is the fact that MQ still realizes 44% of its revenue from Block; this level of revenue concentration poses a risk to topline performance, as Block is looking to partner with more card issuance and payment processing companies.

Conclusion

Despite significant topline and bottom-line growth, solid fundamentals, a healthy balance sheet, and massive growth of the BNPL segment of the business, I don't see any upside from where MQ's stock is currently trading. Management has been vocal about optimizing for GAAP profitability; hence, I expect this news to be already priced in. However, given the volatile environment we are in with massive uncertainties from the macro world, I would suggest readers pay attention to this name and wait for a good entry in case the market pukes.
